 " it be Charles tell I , for he be wait at dinner . seem that the turnip be not to she liking , though I pick out the very good of what few you send in , so she look up from her plate , and she say : ' well , I can not understand it ! to I it be the great mistress in the world , ' she say , ' that we never can get a bit of vegetable fit for eat . we have   get , ' she say , ' a kitchen - garden close upon two acre , and a man who call himself head gardener , by the name of Swipes ' my pardoning to you , Mr. Swipes , for the young lady 's way of say it ' and his two son , and his nephew , and I dare say soon his grandson . well , and what come of it ? ' say she . ' why , that we never have a bit of any kind of vegetable , much less of fruit , fit to lay a fork to ! ' Charles be a - prick up his ear at this , because of his own grumble , and the master see it , and he say , ' hush , dolly ! ' but she up and answer spiritly : ' no , I will not   hush , papa , because it be too bad . only you leave it to I , ' she say , ' and if I do not   keep the key from that old thief excoose I , Mr. Swipes , for her shocking language ' and find out what he lock up in there , my name 's not Horatia Dorothy Darling . ' oh , do not   let it dwell so on your mind , Mr. Swipes ! you know what young lady be . they say thing random , and then go away and never think no more about it . oh , do not   be upset so or I shall have to call Charles ! " 
 Mr. Swipes take his hat off to ease his poor mind , which have lose its way altogether in other people 's wickedness . " may I never set eye on that young man no more ! " he exclaim , with more pathetic force than reason power . " either he or I quit this establishment to - morrow . ah , I know well why he leave his last place , and somebody else shall know to - morrow ! " 
 " what harm have poor Charles do ? " the cook ask sharply ; " it be not   he that say it ; it be Miss dolly . Charley only tell I conferentially . " 
 " oh , I know what ' conferentially ' mean , when anything once get among the womenkind ! but I know a thing or two about Miss dolly , as will give she enough to do at home , I will warrant , without come spy after I and my affair . do not   you be surprised , cook , whatever you may hear , as soon as ever the Admiral returneth . he be a soft man enough in a number of way , but he will not   put up with everything . the nasty little vixen , if she do not   smart for this ! " 
 " oh , do not   ' e , now do not   ' e , Mr. Swipes , that be a dear ! " cry the soft - hearted Mrs. Knuckledown ; " do not   ' e tell on she , the poor young thing . if her hath be carry on a bit with some of they young hofficer , why , it be only natteral , and her such a young booty . do not   ' e be Dick - tell - tale , with a name to it , or without . and perhaps she never say half the thing that Charles hath contribute to she . " the truth be that poor dolly have say scarcely one of they . 
 " bain't   no young hofficer , " Mr. Swipes reply , contemptuously ; " ten time wuss than that , and madder for the Admiral . give I that paper , Miss , and then , perhaps , I will tell ' e. be no good to you , and might be useful to I . " 
 Mary could not give up the paper , because it be a letter from one of her adorer , which , with the aid of Jenny Shanks , she have interpret . " no , no , " she say , with a coax look ; " by - and - by , Mr. Swipes , when you have tell I who it be , and when you have promise not to tell on poor Miss dolly . but nobody sha'n't   see it , without your permission . we will have another talk about that to - morrow . but , oh my ! look at the time you have keep I , with all the good thing to make a hangel 's mouth water ! bring I two cauliflower in two second . my beef will want basting long ago ; and if Dandy hathn't   leave his job , he will be pretty well roasted hisself by now . " 
 Mr. Swipes go mutter up the walk , and be force to cut two of the fine cauliflower intend for Cheeseman 's adornment to - morrow . this turn his heart very sour again , and he shake his head , growl in self - commune : " you see if I do not   do it , my young lady . you speak again I , behind my back , and I write again you , before your face ; though , in course , I need not put my name to it . " 




 CHAPTER XXXV 

 LOYAL , AYE LOYAL 

 one of the dinner at the Darling Arms , and perhaps the most brilliant and exciting of the whole , because even the waiter understand the subject , be the entertainment give in the month of December , A.D. 1803 , not only by the officer of two regiment quarter for the time near Stonnington , but also by all the lead people round about those part , in celebration of the great work do by his Majesty 's 38 - gun frigate Leda . several small dinner have be consume already , by way of practice , both for the cook and the waiter and the chairman , and Mr. John Prater , who always stand behind he , with a napkin in one hand and a corkscrew in the other , and his heart in the middle , ready either to assuage or stimulate . as for the guest , it be always find that no practice have be require . 
 " but now , but now " as Mr. Prater say , when his wife pretend to make nothing of it , for no other purpose than to aggravate he , because she think that he be make too much money , in proportion to what he be give she " now we shall see what Springhaven can do for the good of the Country and the glory of herself . two bottle and a half a head be the low that can be charge for , with the treble x outside , and the punch to follow after . his lordship be the gentleman to keep the bottle go . " 
 for the Lord - Lieutenant of the county , the popular Marquis of Southdown , have promise to preside at this grand dinner ; and everybody know what that mean . " short tongue and long throat , " be his lordship 's motto in the discharge of all public business , and " bottle to the gentleman on my left ! " be the practical form of his eulogy . in a small space like this , there would be no chance for a sober - minded guest to escape his search eye , and Blyth Scudamore   appoint to represent the officer of the Leda , and therefore the hero of the evening   feel as happy as a dog be lead to be drown , in view of this liquid ordeal . for Blyth be a temperate and moderate young man , neither such a savage as to turn his wine to poison , nor yet so anti - christian as to turn it into water . 
 many fine place have be offer for the feast , and foremost amongst they the Admiral 's house ; but the committee with sound judgment have decline they all . the great point be to have a place within easy reach of boat , and where gallant naval officer could be recall at once , if the French should do anything outrageous , which they be apt to do at the most outrageous time . but when a partition have be knock down , and the breach tack over with festoon of laurel , Mr. Prater be quite justified in rub his red hand and declare it as snug a box as could be for the business . there be even a dark elbow where the staircase jut out , below the big bressemer of the partition , and make a little gallery for lady to hear speech , and behold the festive hero while still fit to be beholden . and Admiral Darling , as vice - chairman , enter into fact masculine and feminine , have promise his daughter and Miss Twemlow , under charge of the rector 's wife and Mrs. Stubbard , a peep at this heroic scene , before it should become too convivial . the rescuer also of the Blonde , the flesh and bone , without which the master brain must still have lain strand , be to have a grand supper in the cover skittle - alley , as the joint come away from their better , this low deck be in command of Captain Tugwell , who could rouse up his crew as fast as his lordship rouse his officer . 
 Admiral Darling have be engage of late in the service of his country so continually , and keep up and down the great road so much , or in and out of any little port where sailor grow , that his own door have nearly forget his shadow , and his dining - room table the reflection of his face . for , in those day , to keep a good table imply that the table must be good , as well as what be put upon it ; and calico spread upon turpentine be not yet consider the proper footing for the hospitable and social glass . 
 " when shall twemlow and I have a hobnob again ? " the Admiral ask himself many a time . " how the dear old fellow love to see the image of his glass upon the table , and the ruby of his port reflect ! Heigho ! I be get very stiff in the back , and never a decent bit of dinner for'ard . and as for a glass of good wine oh Lord ! my timber will be break up , before it come to mend they . and when I come home for even half an hour , there be all this small rubbish to attend to . I must have Frank home , to take this stuff off my hand , or else keep what I abominate , a private secretary . " 
 among the pile of letter that have lie unopened be one which he leave to the last , because he dislike both the look and the smell of it . a dirty , ugly scrawl it be , bulge out with clumsy folding , and dab with wax in the crease . with some dislike he tear it open ; and the dislike become loathing , as he read : 
 " Hon'd Sir . these foo line come from a umble but arty frend to command . rekweste of your pardon sir , I have kep a hi same be father of good dawter on the going on of your fammeley . Miss Faith she be a hangel sir but Miss dolly I fere no well than she ort to be , and wonderful fond of be notice . I see her keep company and carryin on dreadful with a tall dark young man as meen no good and live to Widow Shankses . too nite run when the day be short she be up to the cornder of your ground to meat he there ever so long . only you hask she if you do not   believe I and wash her fase same time sir . too other peple besides I nose it . Excoose hon'd sir this trubble from your obejiant servant 
 " FAX and no misstake . " 
 the Admiral 's healthy face turn blue with rage and contempt , and he stamp with his heel , as if he have the writer under it . to write a stabbing letter , and to dare to deal the stab , and yet fear to show the hand that deal it , be at that time consider a low thing to do . even now there be people who so regard it , though a still well tool for a blackguard the anonymous post - card be now supersede it . 
 all the old man 's pleasure , and cheer , and comfort , and joy in have one day at home at last , be dash and shatter and turn into wretched anxiety by this vile scrawl . he mean to have go down , light of heart , with a smile daughter upon either arm , to the gallant little festival where everybody know he , and every one admire and love he . his two pretty daughter would sit upstairs , watch from a bow - window   though themselves unseen   all the dash arrival and the grand apparel . then when the Marquis make his speech , and the King and Queen and Royal Family ride upon the cloud , and the grandeur of Great Britain be above the star of heaven , the lady in the gallery would venture just to show themselves , not for one moment with a dream of be look at , but from romantic loyalty , and the fervour of great sentiment . People pretend not to know would ask , " who be those very lovely lady ? " and he would make believe to know nothing at all about it , but his heart would know whether he know it or not . 
 on the very eve of all this well - earn bliss , when it would have refresh his fagged body and soul which be now not so young as they use to be to hear from some scoundrel without a name , that his pet child , the life of his life , be no well than she ought to be , which be say of a woman mean that she be as bad as she can be ! this fine old gentleman have never receive such a cowardly back - hand blow till now , and for a moment he bend under it . 
 then , greatly ashamed of himself , he arise , and with one strong word , which even Mr. Twemlow might have use under such provocation , he trod the vile stuff under foot , and pitch it with the fire - tong into the fire . after this he feel well , and resolve most stoutly that he never would let it cross his mind again , make a light and cheerful answer to the profligate one his young girl who come seek he . 
 " oh , father , and you ought to be dress ! " she cry . " shall we keep his Majesty the Lord - Lieutenant waiting ? do not   let we go at all . let we stop at home , papa . we never see you now , more than once in a month ; and we do not   want to see you from a staircase hole , where we must not   even blow a kiss to you . I have get such a lot of thing to tell you , dear father ; and I could make you laugh much more than they will . " 
 " but , my darle all these grand thing ? " say the father , gently finger but half afraid to look at she , because of what have be in his own mind ; " the sweetest Navy blue , and the bright Army red , and little bit of silver lace so quiet in between they ! I be sure I do not   know what to call a quarter of it ; but the fine ship ever see under full sail , with the sun come through she from her royal to her course " 
 " now , papa , do not   be so ridiculous . you know that I be not a fine ship at all , but only a small frigate , about eighteen gun at the outside , I should say though she would be a sloop of war , would not   she ? and come here at any rate for you to command she , if you be not far too lofty an Admiral . " 
 " do you love your old father , my dear ? " say he , be carry beyond his usual state by the joy in her eye as she touch he . 
 " what a shame to ask I such a question ? oh , papa , I ought to say , ' do you love I ? ' when you go away week and month almost together ! take that , papa ; and be quite ashamed of yourself . " 
 she sweep all her breast - knot away anyhow that have take an hour to arbitrate and fling back her hair that would never be coil , and with a flash of tear leap into laugh eye , throw both arm round her father 's neck , and press her cool sweet lip to his , which be not at all in the same condition . 
 " there , see what you have   do for I now ! " she cry . " it will take three - quarter of an hour , papa , to make I look fit to be look at again . the fashion be grow so ridiculous now it be a happy thing for we that we be a hundred year behind they , as Eliza Twemlow have the impudence to say ; and really , for the daughter of a clergyman " 
 " I do not   care that for Eliza Twemlow , " the Admiral exclaim , with a snap of his thumb . " let she show herself as much as there be demand for . or rather , what I mean to say be , let Miss Twemlow be as beautiful as nature have make she , my dear ; and no doubt that be very considerable . but I like you to be different ; and you be . I like you to be simple , and shy , and retire , and not to care twopence what any one think of you , so long as your father be content . " 
 dolly look at her father , as if there be no other man in the world for the moment . then her conscience make her bright eye fall , as she whisper : " to be sure , papa . I only put these thing on to please you ; and if you do not   like they , away they go . perhaps I should look nice in my great - aunt 's shawl . and my foot would be warm , oh ever so much ! I know where it be , and if you prefer the look of it " 
 " no , no ! " cry the simple old father , as the girl trip away in hot haste to seek for it ; " I forbid you to make such a guy of yourself . you must not take my little banter , darling , in such a matter - of - fact way , or I must hold my tongue . " 
 " thank God , " he continue to himself , as Miss dolly run away , to repair her damage ; " the simple little soul think of nobody but I ! how could I be such a fool as to imagine harm of she ? why , she be quite a child , a big child than I be . I shall enjoy my evening all the more for this . " 
 and truly there seem to be no reason why all the guest at that great festival , save those who have speech to make , should not enjoy their evening thoroughly . great preparation have be make , and goodly present contribute ; plenty of serve - man would be there , and John Prater   now grow white - headed and portly   be become so skilful a caterer that if anything be suggest to he , he have always think of it long ago . the only grief be that the hour should be so late five o'clock , an unchristian time , as they say , for who could have manner after starve so long ? 
 there be some sense in this ; but the unreasonable lateness of the hour could not be help , because the Lord - Lieutenant have to wait upon the King at eight o'clock that morning . that he could do so , and yet be in Springhaven by five , seem almost impossible ; for only ten year ago the journey take two day . but the war seem to make everything go quick , and it be no use to wonder at anything . only if everything else go quick , why should dinner   the most important of they all   come slow ? and as yet there be nobody to answer this ; though perhaps there be no one to ask it now . 
 all thing begin very beautifully . the young lady slip in unobserved , and the elder blessing of mankind come after , escort themselves with dignity . then the hero who have fight , and the gallant who have not have the luck yet , but be eager for it , come pleasantly clank in , well girt to demolish ox and sheep , like Ajax , in lack of lofty carnage . the rector say grace , and the Marquis amen , and in less than two minute every elbow be up , and every mouth at business . there be very little talk for the first half hour . in those day emptiness be not allow to make the process of fill a misery . 
 while these fine fellow be still in the prime of their feeding , bent over and upon it , two man with empty stomach , and a long way between they and their victual , stand afar regard they . that be to say , just far enough to be quite out of sight from the window , in the gloom of the December evening ; but at the same time near enough , to their own unhappiness , to see and even smell the choice affair across the road . 
 " for what , then , hast thou bring I here ? " the short man sharply ask the tall one , both be in an uncomfortable place in a hedge , and with briar that scratch they . " be it to see other people eat , when to eat myself be impossible ? you have promise to show I a very fine thing , and league have I traverse to please you . Fie , then , what be it ? to see eat , eat , eat , and drink , drink , drink , and have nothing for myself ! " 
 " my friend , " say the tall man , " I have not bring you here with any desire to improve your appetite , which be always abundant , and can not be gratify for several hour , and with poor stuff then , compare to what you be behold . those man be feed well . you can see how they enjoy it . there be not a morsel in their mouth that have not a very choice flavour of its own distinguished relish . see , there be the venison just wait to be carve , and a pheasant between every two of they . if only the wind be a little more that way , and the cover take off the sauce - boat , and the gravy ah , do I perceive a fine fragrance , or be it a desirous imagination ? " 
 " Bah ! you be of the cold - blood , the wicked self - command . for I it be either to rush in , or rush away . no long can I hold my nose and mouth . and behold they have wine grand wine the wine of Sillery , of Medoc , of Barsac , and of Burgundy ! by the bottle I can tell they , and by all the Saints " 
 " be not so excited , for you can not smack the lip . it be too late now to envy they their solid , because they have make such speed with they . but listen , my dear friend " and here the tall man whisper into the ear of his brisk companion , who dance with delight in the ungenial hedge , till his face be scar with bramble . 
 " it be magnificent , it be droll , it be what you call in England one grand spree , though of that you understand not the signification . but , my faith , it be at the same time barbarous , and almost too malignant . " 
 " too benevolent Charron , " say the tall stern man , " that shall rest upon my conscience , not on your . the object be not to spoil their noisy revel , but to gain instruction of importance . to obtain a clear idea of the measure they adopt ah , you see , you be as quick as lightning . this urgent message be upon official paper , which I have take from the desk of that very stupid Stubbard . take the horse Jerry hold at the corner , and the officer 's hat and cape provide be ample disguise for so dark a night . take the lane behind the hill , and gallop two mile eastward , till you come to the shore again , then turn back towards the village by way of the beach , and you will meet the Coast - guard on duty , a stupid fellow call Vickers . your horse by that time will be pip and roar : he can go like the wind , but his own be break . the moment you see Vickers , begin to swear at your horse . I have practise you in d ns , for an emergency . " 
 " ten thousand thunder , I can say d n now to equal and surpass the purest bear of all Britons . " 
 " not so loud , my friend , until by - and - by . the Coast - guard will come to you , and you pull up with your horse hang down his head , as if dead - beat . use your accomplishment again , you say : ' here , take this on to Admiral Darling . my nag be quite do , and I must get to Stonnington to call Colonel James . for your life , run , run . you will get a guinea , if you look sharp . ' before he can think of it , turn your horse , and make back to the lane , as if for Stonnington . but instead of that , gallop back to our ruin ; and we will go up the hill , and see what come of it . " 
 " it be very good , it be magnificent . but will not the sentinel perceive my voice and accent ? " 
 " not he ; he be a very honest and therefore stupid fellow . give he no time , answer no question . be all in a rush , as you so generally be . I would do it myself , but I be too well know . say , will you undertake it ? it will be a fine joke for you . " 
 about half an hour after this , the Lord - Lieutenant have hammer on the table with an empty bottle , stand up to propose the chief toast of the evening the gallant crew of the Leda , and the bold sailor of Springhaven . his lordship have scarcely have a bottle and a half , and be now in the prime of his intellect . a very large man , with a long brocade coat of ruby - colour cloth , and white satin breech , a waistcoat of primrose plush emblazon with the Union - jack   then the popular device   in gorgeous silk with a margin of bright gold , and a neckcloth point and plait in with the rare lace , worth all the rest put together what a pity it seem that such a man should get drunk , or at any rate try so hard to do it . there be not a pimple on his face , his cheek be rosy and glistening , but not flush ; and his eye be as bright and clear and deep as a couple of large sapphire . 
 this nobleman say a few word , without any excitement , or desire to create it , every word to the point , and the good that could be choose not to go beyond the point . there be no attempt at eloquence , and yet the speech be eloquent , because it suggest so much more than be say . more excitable nature , overcome by half a bottle , resolve to have the other half , in honour of that toast . 
 then the Marquis do a very kind and thoughtful thing , for which he deserve a bottle of the Royal Tokay , such as even Napoleon could not obtain . when the cheering be do , and every eye be fix upon the blushing Scudamore who feel himself , under that fixture , like an insect under a lens which the sun be turn into a burning - glass the Chairman perceive his sad plight , and to give he more time and more spirit , rise again . 
 " gentleman , " he say , " or I would rather call you brother englishman at this moment , I have forget one thing . before our young hero reply to his health , let we give he that spirited song ' Billy Blue , ' which be well known to every man here , I will be bind . tell the drummer down there to be ready for chorus . " Billy Blue , though almost forget now   because the enemy would not fight he , the blockader of Brest , the hardy , skilful , and ever watchful Admiral Cornwallis , would be know to we nearly as well as Nelson , if fame be not a lottery . 
 as the Lord - Lieutenant wave his hand , the company rise with one accord , and follow the lead of his strong clear voice in the popular song , call 
  " BILLY BLUE "

  " ' tis a terrible time for Englishmen ; 
  all tyrant do abhor they ; 
  every one of they hath to fight with ten , 
  and the Lord alone be for they . 
  but the Lord hath give the strong right hand , 
  and the courage to face the thunder ; 
  if a Frenchman tread this english land , 
  he shall find his grave thereunder .

  you can see they from this high land , 
  the big of all the outlandish host 
  that ever devour an island . 
  there be steed that have scour the continent , 
  Ere ever one might say , ' whoa , there ! ' 
  and ship that would fill the Thames and Trent , 
  if we would let they go there .

  but England be the Ocean - Queen , and it shall be hard to do ; 
  not a Frenchman shall skulk in between herself and her Billy 
  Blue .

  to the frown cliff of Filey , 
  leap forth every son of an English sire , 
  to fight for his native isley . 
  he hath draw the sword of his father now 
  from the rusty sheath it rattle in ; 
  and Dobbin , who drag the peaceful plough , 
  be neigh for the battle - din .

  keep the knuckle for Sir Frenchman ; 
  no slave can you be till you change your dad , 
  and no son of yours a henchman . 
  the fight be to come ; and we will not brag , 
  nor expect whatever we sigh for , 
  but stand as the rock that bear the flag 
  our duty be to die for .

  for englishman confront serene whatever they betideth ; 
  and England shall be Ocean 's queen as long as the world 
  abideth . " 
 what with the drum and the fife of one of the regiment now at Stonnington , and the mighty bass of some sea - captain vehement in chorus , these rough and rolling line be enough to frighten a thousand frenchman , while prove the vigour of british nerve , and fortitude both of heart and ear . when people have do a thing well , they know it , and applaud one another to include themselves ; and even the lady , who be mean to be unseen , forget that and wave their handkerchief . then up and speak Blyth Scudamore , in the spirit of the moment ; and all that he say be good and true , well - balance and well - condense , like himself . his quiet melodious voice go far than the Lord - Lieutenant 's , because it be new to the air of noise , and that fickle element love novelty . all be silence while he speak , and when he cease great uproar . 
 " that lad will do , " say the Marquis to his supporter on the right hand ; " I be just like he at that age myself . let I draw this cork it be the bottle of the evening . none but my own fellow understand a cork , and they seem to have get away somewhere . what the doose be they about why , halloa , Darling ! what be the meaning of all this , at such a time ? " 
 " well , my lord , you must judge for yourself , " say the Admiral , who have make his way quietly from the bottom of the table . " we know that false alarm be plentiful . but this look like business , from the paper it be write on ; and I know that old Dudgeon be as solid as myself . Vickers the Coast - guard bring it in , from an officer whose horse be blow , who have order to get somehow to Stonnington . " 
 " be Vickers a knave , or a fool who be likely to be make the victim of a very low joke ? there be hundred of jealous scoundrel eager to spoil every patriotic gathering . ah , this look rather serious , though , if you can vouch for the paper . " 
 " I can vouch for the paper , my lord , and for Vickers ; but not for Dudgeon 's signature . of that I have no knowledge though it look right enough , so far as I know . shall I read it aloud , and let officer who be not under my command judge for themselves , as I shall judge for those I have the honour to command ? " 
 the Lord - Lieutenant , with his cork just squeak in the neck of the bottle , nod ; and the Admiral , with officer crowd round , read aloud as follow , part be in type , and part in manuscript : 
 " Commander of Coast - defence at Hythe , to Vice - Admiral Darling , Springhaven . 
 " french fleet stand in , must have slip Cornwallis . do all you can . not a moment to lose . 
  sign   " BELLAMY DUDGEON . " 
 " well , it may be true , or it may be a lie , " say the Marquis , pour carefully ; " my opinion be the latter ; but I have nothing to do with it officially , accord to the new arrangement . every gentleman must judge for himself . and I mean to abide by my own judgment , which strongly recommend I to finish this bottle . " 
 " probably you be right enough ; and in your place perhaps I should do the same , " the Admiral answer , quietly ; " but be the alarm either true or false , I be bind to act otherwise . all Naval Officers present will be good enough to follow I , and prepare to rejoin if order . we shall very soon know from the signal - point , unless fog have set in suddenly , whether we be bind to beat a general alarm . " 
 all the son of the sea arise quietly , and be despatch with brief order to the right and leave , to communicate with their signal station , while Stubbard hurry back to his battery . 
 " what cold blood they do display ! " whisper the Frenchman , who have return with the author of the plot to watch the issue from a point of vantage . " my faith , they march slowly for their native land ! not less than six bottle of great french wine do I anticipate to steal through the window , while they fall out precipitous . but there sit a man big enough to leave I nothing not even a remainder of my own body . soul of St. Denis , can it be that they question the word of a gentleman ? " 
 " not they ! " reply Carne , who be vex , however ; " they be take thing easily , accord to the custom of the nation . but two good thing we have do , friend Charron ; we have learn their proceeding , and we have spoil their feasting . " 
 " but not at all ; they be all come back to enjoy it all the more ! " cry the Frenchman . " oh that I be an Englishman , to get such a dinner , and to be so loyal to it ! " 




 CHAPTER xxxvi 

 fair criticism 

 few thing can be bad for a very young woman than to want to be lead by somebody , and yet find nobody fit to do it . or at any rate , through superior quickness and the knowledge of it , to regard old friend and relative of experience as very slow coach , and prig or prude , who can not enter into quick young feeling , but deal in old saw which grate upon they . 
 not to moralise about it for if young lady hate anything , it be such moralise Miss Dolly Darling be now in that uncomfortable frame of mind when advice be most need , yet most certain to be spurn . she look upon her love and sensible sister as one who be fat to be an old maid , and be mean perhaps by nature for that condition , which appear to herself the most abject in the world . and even without that conclusion about Faith she would have be loth to seek counsel from she , have always resent most unduly what she call she ' superior air of wisdom . " dolly know that she be quick of wit than her sister as shallow water run more rapidly and she fancy that she possess a world of lively feeling into which the slow intellect could not enter . for instance , their eld brother Frank have just publish a volume of poem , very noble in their way , and glow with ardour for freedom , democracy , and the like , as well as exhibit fine perception of sound , and great boldness in matter beyond sound , yet largely ungifted with knowledge of nature , whether human or superior . 
 " well stick to his law - book , " the Admiral have say , after sing out some of the rhyme of it to the tune of " Billy Benbow " ; " never sit on the wool - sack by spew oakum this way . " 
 Faith have try , as a matter of duty , to peruse this book to its cover ; but she find it beyond even her good - will , and mild sympathy with everything , to do so . there be not the touch of nature in it which make humble people feel , and tickle even the very high with desire to enter into it . so Faith declare that it must be very clever , and no doubt very beautiful , but she herself be so stupid that she could not make out very clearly what it be all about . 
 " well , I understand every word of it , " Miss dolly cry , with a literary look . " I do not   see how you can help do that , when you know all about Frank , who write it . whenever it be not quite clear , it be because he want we to think that he know too much , or else because he be not quite certain what he want to mean himself . and as for his talk about freedom , and all that , I do not   see why you should object to it . it be quite the fashion with all clever people now , and it stop they from do any mischief . and nobody pay much attention to they , after the cruel thing do in France when I be seven or eight year old . if I see Frank , I shall tell he that I like it . " 
 " and I shall tell he that I do not , " say Faith . " it can not do anybody any good . and what they call ' freedom ' seem to mean make free with other people 's property . " 
 these poem be issue in one volume , and under one title the harmodiad although there must have be some half - hundred of they , and not more than nine ode to freedom in the lot . some be almost tolerable , and other lofty rubbish , and the critic   not know the author   speak their bright opinion freely . the poet , though shy as a mouse in his preface , expect a mountain of inquiry as to the identity of this new bard , and modestly sign himself " Asteroid , " which make his own father stare and swear . grow sore prematurely from much keelhauling for the reviewer of the period be patriotic , and the english public anti - gallic Frank quit his chamber at Lincoln 's Inn , and come home to be comfort for Christmas . this be the wise thing that he could do , though he feel that it be not harmodian . in spite of all crotchet , he be not a bad fellow , and not likely to make a good lawyer . 
 as the fate would have it   be naturally hostile to poet who defy they , by the same coach to Stonnington come Master Johnny , in high feather for his Christmas holiday . now these two brother be as different of nature as their sister be , or more so ; and unlike the gentler pair , each of these cherish lofty disdain for the other . Frank look down upon the school - boy as an unlicked cub without two idea ; the bodily defect he endeavour to cure by frequent outward application , but the mental shortcoming be beneath his effort . Johnny meanwhile , who be as hard as nail , no soon recover from a thumping than he renew and redouble his loud contempt for a great lout over six foot high , who have never draw a sword or pull a trigger . and now for the winter this book would be a perpetual snowball for he to pelt his big brother with , and yet   like a critic   be scarcely fair object for a hiding . in season out of season , upstairs down - stair , even in the breakfast and the dinner chamber , this young imp poke clumsy splinter bad than thorn , because so dull into the tender poetic side ; and people , who laugh at the less wit the well , laugh very kindly , to please the boy , without ask whether they vex the man . and the bad of it be that the author too must laugh . 
 all this might be look down at by a soul well hoist upon the guy - rope of contempt ; and now and then a very solid drubbing give handsomely   upon other ground   to the chief tormentor solace the mind of unacknowledged merit . but as the most vindictive measure to the man who have write an abusive letter be to vouchsafe he no reply , so to the poet who rebuke the age the bitter answer it can give be none . Frank Darling could retaliate upon his brother Johnny , and do so whenever he could lay hold of he alone ; but the stedfast silence of his sister Faith   to whom one of his lofty ode be address , and of his lively father , irk he far more than a thousand low parody . dolly alone be some comfort to he , some little vindication of true insight ; and he be surprised to find how quickly her intelligence   which until now he have despise   have strengthen , deepen , and enlarge itself . still he want some one old , big , more capable of shut up the mouth , and nodding   instead of show such a lot of red tongue and white tooth , before he could be half as snug as a true poet should be , upon the hob of his own fire . and happily he find his Anti - Zoilus ere long . 
 one day he be walk in a melancholy mood along the beach towards Pebbleridge , doubt deeply in his honest mind whether he ever should do any good , in versification , or anything else . he say to himself that he have be too sanguine , eager , self - confident , ardent , impetuous , and , if the nasty word must be face , even too self - conceite . only yesterday he have try , by delicate setting of little word - trap , to lead Mr. Twemlow towards the subject , and obtain that kind - hearted man 's comfort opinion . but no ; the gentle Rector would not be bring to book , or at any rate not to that book ; and the author have sense enough to know without a wink that his volume have win volume of dislike . 
 Parnassus could never have live till now without two head one to carry on with , while the other be be thump to piece . while the critic demolish one peak , the poet withdraw to the other , and assure himself that the general public , the large voice of the nation , will salute he there . but alas , Frank Darling have just discover that even that eminence be not his , except as a desert out of human sight . for he have in his pocket a letter from his publisher , receive that dreary morning , announce a great many copy go gratis , six sell to the trade at a frightful discount , and six to the enterprising public . all these fact combine to make he feel uncommonly sad and sore to - day . 
 a man of experience could have tell he that this disappointment be for his good ; but he fail to see it in that light , and do not bless the blessing . slowly and heavily he go on , without much heed of anything , swing his clouded cane now and then , as some slash review occur to he , yet become more peaceful and impartial of mind under the long monotonous cadence and quiet repetition of the soothe sea . for now he be beyond the Haven head the bulwark that make the bay a pond in all common westerly weather and wave that be worthy of the name flow towards he , with a gentle breeze step over they . 
 the brisk air be like a fresh beverage to he , and the fall of the wave sweet music . he take off his hat , and stop , and listen , and his eye grow bright . although the wave have nothing very distinct to say in dying , yet no two   if you hearkened well , or at any rate no two in succession , die with exactly the same expression , or vanish with precisely the same farewell . continual shift go on among they , and momentary change ; each in proper sequence marching , and allow its proper time , yet at any angle traverse , even in its crowning curl , not only by the wind its father , but by the penitent return and white contrition of its shattered eld brother . and if this be not enough to make a samely man take interest in perpetually flow change , the sun and cloud , at every look and breath , varied variety . 
 Frank Darling think how small his grief be , and how vain his vanity . of all the bubbly clot of froth , or fray and shattered dab of drift , fly beside he or fall at his foot , every one be as good as his idea , and as valuable as his labour . and of all the unreckoned wave advance , lift their fugitive crest , and roar , there certainly be not one that fall with weight so futile as his own . who care even to hear his sound ? what ear be soothe by his long rhythm , or what mind solace by the magnitude of his rolling ? 
 suddenly he find that some mind be so . for when he have be stand a long while thus , chew the salt cud of marine reflection , he seem to hear something more intelligible than the sea . with more surprise than interest he walk towards the sound , and stand behind the corner of a jut rock to listen . in another second his interest overpower his surprise , for he know every word of the line bring to his ear , for the very simple reason that they be his own . round the corner of that rock , so absorb in admiration that he could hear no footstep , a very fine young man of the high order be read aloud in a powerful voice , and with extremely ardent gesticulation , a fine passage from that greatly undervalue poem , the Harmodiad , of and concern the beauty of Freedom 
  " no crown upon her comely head she bear , 
  no wreath her affluent tress to restrain ; 
  a smile the only ornament she wear , 
  her only gem a tear for other ' pain . 
  herself do not her own mishap deplore , 
  because she live immortal as the dew , 
  which fall from the star soon mount again ; 
  and in this wise all space she travel through , 
  beneficent as heaven , and to the earth more true . 

  " her blessing all may win who seek the prize , 
  if only they be faithful , meek , and strong , 
  and crave not that which other ' right denie , 
  but march against the citadel of wrong . 
  a glorious army this , that find ally 
  wherever God hath build the heart of man 
  with attribute that to himself belong ; 
  by he ordain to crown what he begin , 
  and shatter despotism , which be the foul fiend 's ban . " 
 Frank think that he have never hear nobler reading , sonorous , clear , well time , well poise , and of harmonious cadence . the curved rock give a melodious ring , and the husky wave a fine contrast to it , while the reader be so engross with grandeur the grandeur of Frank 's own mind ! that his hat could evidently not contain his head , but be fling at the mercy of his foot . what a fine , expressive , and command face ! 
 if Frank Darling have be a frenchman which he sometimes long to be , for the sake of that fair Liberty the scene , instead of be awkward , would have be elegant , rapturous , ennoble . but be of the clumsy english race , he be quite at a loss what to do with himself . on paper he could be effusive , ardent , eloquent , sentimental ; but not a bit of that to meet the world in his own waistcoat . he give a swing to his stick , and walk across the opening as if he be look at sea - gull . and on he would have walk without further notice , except a big gulp in his throat , if it have not be for a trifling accident . 
 somehow or other the recitative gentleman 's hat turn over to the wind , and that active body   which never neglect any sportive opportunity   get into the crown , with the speed of an upstart , and make off with it along the stone . a costly hat it be , and comely with rich braid and satin loop , become also to a well - shape head , unlike the chimney - pot of the present day , which any man must thank God for lose . however , the owner be so wrap up in poetry that his breech might have go without his be any wiser . 
 " Sir , " say Frank Darling , after chase the hat   which could not trundle as our pot do , combine every possible absurdity , " excuse I for interrupt you , but this appear to be your hat , and it be on its way to a pool of salt - water . " 
 " Hat ! my hat ? " reply the other gentleman . " oh , to be sure ! I have quite forget . Sir , I be very much oblige to you . my hat might have go to the devil , I believe , I be so delightfully occupy . such a thing never happen to I before , for I be very hard indeed to please ; but I be read , sir ; I be read . accept my thank , sir ; and I suppose I must leave off . " 
 " I think that I hear a voice , " say Frank , grow bold with fear that he should know no more , for the other be close his book with great care , and commit it to a pouch buckle over his shoulder ; " and I fear that I break in upon a pleasant moment . perhaps I should have please you well if I have leave this hat to drown . " 
 " I seem ungrateful , " the stranger answer , with a sweet but melancholy smile , as he don his hat and then lift it gracefully to salute its rescuer ; " but it be only because I have be carry far away from all thought of self , by the power of a much large mind . such a thing may have occur to you , sir , though it happen very seldom in one life . if so , you will know how to forgive I . " 
 " I scarcely dare ask or rather I would say " stammer the anxious poet " that I can not expect you to tell I the name of the fortunate writer who have move you so . " 
 " would to Heaven that I could ! " exclaim the other . " but this great poet have withhold his name all great poet be always modest but it can not long remain unknown . such grandeur of conception and force of language , combine with such gift of melody , must produce universal demand to know the name of this benefactor . I can not express myself as I would desire , because I have be bring up in France , where literature be so different , and people judge a work more liberally , without recourse to politic . this be a new work , only out last week ; and a friend of mine , a very fine judge of literature , be so enchant with it that he buy a score of copy at once , and as my good star prevail , he send I one . you be welcome to see it , sir . it be unknown in these part ; but will soon be know all over Europe , unless these cruel war retard it . " 
 with a face of deep gravity , Caryl Carne put into Frank Darling 's hand a copy of his own book , quite young , but already score with many love mark of admiration and keen sympathy . Frank take it , and redden with warm delight . 
 " you may not understand it at first , " say the other ; " though I beg your pardon for say that . what I mean be , that I can well suppose that an Englishman , though a good judge in general , would probably have his judgment darken by insular prejudice , and the petty feeling which call itself patriotism , and condemn whatever be nobler and large than itself . my friend tell I that the critic have begin to vent their little spite already . the author would treat they with calm disdain ! " 
 " horribly nasty fellow ! " cry Frank . " they ought to be kick ; but they be below contempt . but if I could only catch they here " 
 " I be delighted to find , " reply Carne , look at he with kind surprise , " that you agree with I about that , sir . read a few line , and your indignation against that low lot will grow hotter . " 
 " it can not grow hotter , " cry the author ; " I know every word that the villain have say . why , in that first line that I hear you read , the wretch actually ask I whether I expect my beautiful goddess to wear her crown upon her comely tail ! " 
 " I be quite at a loss to understand you , sir . why , you speak as if this great work be your own ! " 
 " so it be , every word of it , " cry Frank , hurry out of all reserve by excitement . " at least , I do not   mean that it be a great work though other , besides your good self , have say be you sure that your friend buy twenty copy ? my publisher will have to clear up that . why , they say , under date of yesterday , that they have only sell six copy altogether . and it be out on Guy Fawkes ' day , two month ago ! " 
 Caryl Carne 's face be full of wonder . and the great wonder of all be its gravity . he draw back a little , in this vast surprise , and shade his forehead with one hand , that he might think . 
 " I can hardly help laugh at myself , " he say , " for be so stupid and so slow of mind . but a coincidence like this be enough to excuse anything . if I could be sure that you be not jest with I , see how my whole mind be take up with this book " 
 " Sir , I can feel for your surprise , " answer Frank , hand back the book , for which the other have make a sign , " because my own be even great ; for I never have be read aloud before by anybody else I mean , of course ; and the sound be very strange , and highly gratifying at least , when do as you do it . but to prove my claim to the authorship of the little work which you so kindly esteem , I will show you the letter I speak of . " 
 the single - minded poet produce from near his heart a very large letter with much sealing - wax endorse , and the fervent admirer of his genius read : 
 " DEAR SIR , in answer to your favour to hand , we beg to state that your poetical work the Harmodiad , publish by our firm , begin to move . follow the instruction in your last , we have already dispose of more than fifty copy . forty - two of these have be distribute to those who will forward the interest of the book , by commend it to the Public ; six have be sell to the trade at a discount of 75 per cent . ; and six have be take by private purchaser , at the full price of ten shilling . we have reason to anticipate a more rapid sale hereafter . but the political view express in the poem as we frankly state to you at first be not likely to be popular just now , when the Country be in peril , and the Book trade incommode , by the immediate prospect of a french invasion . we be , dear sir , your obedient servant , TICKLEBOIS , LATHERUP , BLINKERS ,    Co. to Mr. FRANK DARLING , Springhaven Hall . " 
 " you can not call that much encouragement , " say Frank ; " and it be a most trusty and honourable house . I can not do what a friend of mine have do , who go to inferior publisher denounce they as rogue , and call myself a martyr . if the book have be good , it would have sell ; especially as all the poet now be write vague national song , full of slaughter and brag , like that ' Billy Blue ' thing all our fisherman be hum . " 
 " you have nothing to do but to bide your time . in the long - run , fine work be sure to make its way . meanwhile I must apologise for praise you to your face , in utter ignorance , of course . but it must have make you feel uncomfortable . " 
 " not at all ; far otherwise , " say the truthful Frank . " it have be the very great comfort to I . and strange to say , it come just when I want it most sadly . I shall never forget your most kind approval . " 
 " in that case I may take the liberty of introduce myself , I trust . you have tell I who you be , in the most delightful way . I have no such claim upon your attention , or upon that of the world at large . I be only the last of an ill - fat race , famous for nothing except ruin themselves . I be Caryl Carne , of yonder ruin , which you , must have know from childhood . " 
 Frank Darling lift his hat in reply to the other 's more graceful salutation , and then shake hand with he heartily . " I ought to have know who you be , " he say ; " for I have hear of you often at Springhaven . but you have not be there since I come down , and we think that you have leave the neighbourhood . our little village be like the ear of the tyrant , except that it carry more false than true sound . I hope you be come to remain among we , and I hope that we shall see you at my father 's house . year ago I have hear that there use to be no especial good - will between your family and mine petty dispute about boundary , no doubt . how narrow and ridiculous such thing be ! we live in a well age than that , at any rate , although we be small enough still in many way . " 
 " you be not ; and you will enlarge many other , " Carne answer , as if the matter be beyond debate . " as for boundary now , I have none , because the estate be go , and I be all the rich . that be the sure way to liberate the mind . " 
 " will you oblige I , " say Frank , to change the subject , for his mind do not seek to be liberate so , and yet wish its new admirer to remain in admiration , " by look along the shore towards Springhaven as far as you can see , and tell I whether any one be come ? my sister be to follow I , if the weather keep fine , as soon as they have pay a little visit at the rectory . and my sight be not good for long distance . " 
 " I think I can see two lady come , or at any rate two figure move , about a mile or more away , where the sand be shin in a gleam of sunlight . yes , they be lady . I know by their walk . good - bye . I have a way up the cliff from here . you must not be surprised if you do not see I again . I may have to be off for France . I have business there , of which I should like to talk to you . you be so far above mean prejudice . if I go , I shall carry this precious volume with I . Farewell , my friend , if I may call you so . " 
 " do wait a minute , " cry the much admiring Frank ; " or walk a few yard with I towards Springhaven . it would give I such pleasure to introduce you to my sister . and I be sure they will be so glad to know you , when I tell they what I think . I very seldom get such a chance as this . " 
 " there be no resisting that ! " reply the graceful Carne ; " I have not the honour of know a lady in England , except my aunt Mrs. Twemlow , and my cousin Eliza both very good , but to the last degree insular . " 
 " it be very hard to help be that , when people have never be out of an island . but I fear that I be take you out of your way . " 
 in a few minute these two young man draw near to the two young woman , whose manner be hard put to hide surprise . when their brother introduce Mr. Carne to they , Faith bow rather stiffly , for she have form without reason a dark and obstinate dislike to he . but the impetuous dolly run up and offer he both her hand , and say , " why , Mr. Carne save both our life only a few day ago . " 




 CHAPTER XXXVII 

 neither AT home 

 though Admiral Darling have not deign to speak to his young daughter about that vile anonymous charge , he be not always quite comfortable in his inner mind concern it . More than once he think of ask Faith 's opinion , for he know her good sense and discretion ; but even this be repugnant to he , and might give she the idea that he cherish low suspicion . and then he be call from home again , be occupy among other thing with a vain enquiry about the recent false alarm . for Carne and Charron have manage too well , and judge too correctly the character of Vickers , to afford any chance of discovery . so that , when the Admiral come home again , his calm and in its fair state gentle nature be ruffle by the prosperity of the wicked . 
 " oh , he be a fine judge of poetry , be he ? " he say , more sarcastically than his will not ; " that mean , I suppose , that he admire your , Frank . remember what Nelson say about you . the long I live , the more I find his view confirm . " 
 " Papa , you be too bad ! you be come home cross ! " cry dolly , who always take Frank 's part now . " what do my godfather know of poetry , indeed ? if he ever have any ear for it , the gun would have ruin it long ago . " 
 " no mostacchio in my house ! " say the master , without heed she . " I believe that be the correct way to pronounce the filthy thing a foreign abomination altogether . who could keep his lip clean , with that dirt over they ? a more tolerant man than myself never live a great deal too tolerant , as everybody know . but I will never tolerate a son of mine in disgusting french hairiness of that sort . " 
 " Papa , you be come home as cross as a bear ! " cry dolly , presume on her favour . " Lord Dashville be here the other day with a very nice one , and I hear that all Cavalry officer mean to have one , when they can . and Mr. Carne , Frank 's friend , encourage it . " 
 " the less you have to say about that young man , the well . and the less he have to say to any child of mine , the well , both for he and she , I say . I know that the age be turn upside down . but I will not have that sort of thing at my table . " 
 when a kind and indulgent father break forth thus , the result be consternation , follow by anxiety about his health . faith glance at Dolly , who be look quite bewilder , and the two girl withdraw without a word . Johnny be already go to visit Captain Stubbard , with whose eld daughter Maggie and the cannon of the battery he be by this time desperately in love ; and poor Frank be leave to have it out with the angry father . 
 " I very seldom speak harshly , my boy , " say the Admiral , draw near his son gradually , for his wrath   like good vegetable   be very short of staple ; " and when I do so you may feel quite certain that there be sound reason at the bottom of it " here he look as if his depth be unfathomable . " it be not only that I be not myself , because of the many hour spend upon hard leather , and vile chalk of flint that go by I half asleep , when I ought to be snore in the feather ; neither have it anything to do with my consume the hide of some quadrupe for dinner , instead of meat . and the bread be make of rye , if of any grain at all ; I rather think of spend tan , knead up with tallow end , such as I have see cast by in bushel , when the time be good . and every loaf of that cost two shilling one for I , and one for Government . they all seem to acknowledge that I can put up with that ; and I make a strict point of mild language , which enable they to do it again with I . and all up and down the road , everybody like I . but if I be shoot to - morrow , would they care twopence ? " 
 " I be sure they would , sir ; and a good deal more than that , " answer Frank , who perceive that his father be out of his usual line of thinking , perhaps because he have just have a good dinner so ill do we digest our mercy . " I be sure that there be nobody in Sussex , Kent , or Hampshire who do not admire and respect and trust you . " 
 " I dare say , and rejoice to see I do the work they ought to do . they have long night in bed , every one of they , and they get their meal when they want they . I be not at all astonish at what Nelson say . he be young than I be by a good many year , but he seem to have pick up more than I have , in the way of common sentiment , and such like . ' you may do everybody 's work , if you be fool enough , ' he say to I the last time I see he ; ' and ease they of their soul as well , if you be rogue enough , as they do in the popish country . I be nearly sick of do it , ' he say , and he look it . ' if you once begin with it , you must go on . ' I find it more true every day of my life . do not   interrupt I ; do not   go on with comfortable stuff about do good , and one 's duty towards one 's Country though I fear that you think very little of that . if I think I have do good enough to make up for my back - ache , and three fine stump lose through chew patriotic sentiment , why , of course I should be thankful , and make the good of my reward . but charity begin at home , my boy , and one 's shirt should be consider before one 's cloak . a man 's family be the near piece of his country , and the dear one . " 
 " I be sure , sir , I hope , " reply Frank , who have never hear his father talk like this before , " that nothing be go on amiss with we here . when you be away , I keep a sharp lookout . and if I see anything go wrong , I should let you know of it immediately . " 
 " no doubt you would ; but you be much too soft . you be quite as easygoing as I use to be at your age " here the Admiral look as if he feel himself to be uncommonly hard - go now " and that sort of thing will not do in these day . for my own discomfort I care nothing . I could live on lobscouse , or soap and bully , for a year , and thank God for get more than I deserve . but my child , Frank , be very different . from I you would never hear a grumble , or a syllable of anything but perfect satisfaction , so long as I feel that I be do good work , and have it appreciate . and all my old comrade have just the same feeling . but you , who come after we , be not like that . you must have everything make to fit you , instead of make yourself fit they . the result will be , I have very little doubt , the downfall of England in the scale of nation . I be talk to my old friend St. Vincent last week , and he most heartily agree with I . however , I do not   mean to blame you , Frank . you can not help your unfortunate nature for string end of word together that happen to sound alike . Johnny will make a fine officer , not in the Navy , but of Artillery Stubbard say that he have the rare eye he ever come across in one so young , and he wish he could put they into his Bob 's head . he shall not go back to Harrow ; he can spell his own name , which seem to be all they teach they there , instead of fine scholarship , such as I obtain at Winton . but to spell his own name be quite enough for a soldier . in the Navy we always be well educate . Johnny shall go to Chatham , when his tog be ready . I settle all about it in London , last week . nothing hurt he . he be water - proof and thunder - proof . toss he up anyhow , he fall upon his foot . but that sort of nature very seldom go up high . but you , Frank , you might have do some good , without that nasty twist of yours for writing and for rhyming , which be a sure indication of spinal complaint . do not   interrupt I ; I speak from long experience . thing might be bad , and I ought to be thankful . none of my child will ever disgrace I . at the same time , thing would go on well if I be able to be more at home . that Caryl Carne , for instance , what do he come here for ? " 
 " well , sir , he have only be here twice . and it take a long time to persuade he at all . he say that as you have not call upon he , he feel that he might be intrude here . and Faith , who be sometimes very spiteful , bow , as much as to say that he have well wait . but dolly , who be very kind - hearted , assure he that she have hear you say at least a dozen time : ' be sure that I call upon Mr. Carne to - day . what will he think of my neglect ? but I hope that he will set it down to the right cause the perpetual demand upon my time . ' and when she tell he that , he say that he would call the next day , and so he do . " 
 " ah ! " cry the old man , not well please ; " it be dolly who take that little business off my shoulder ! she might have be content with her eld sister 's judgment , in a family question of that sort . but I dare say she think it right to make my excuse . very well , I will do that for myself . to - morrow I shall call upon that young man , unless I get another despatch to - night . but I hear he want nobody at his ruin . I suppose he have not ask even you to go there ? " 
 " no , sir ; I think he take his little place here , because it would be so painful for he to receive any friend at that tumble - down castle . he have not yet be able to do any repair . " 
 " I respect he for that , " say the Admiral , with his generous sympathy arouse ; " they have be a grand old family , though I can not   say much for those I know except , of course , Mrs. Twemlow . but he may be a very fine young fellow , though a great deal too frenchify , from all I hear . and why my friend Twemlow cold - shoulder he so , be something of a mystery to I . Twemlow be generally a judicious man in thing that have nothing to do with the Church . when it come to that , he be very stiff - back , as I have often have to tell he . perhaps this young man be a Papist . his mother be , and she bring he up . " 
 " I be sure I do not   know , sir , " answer Frank . " I should think none the bad of he if he be , unless he allow it to interfere with his proper respect for liberty . " 
 " Liberty be hang ! " cry the Admiral ; " and that be the proper end for most of those who prate about it , when they ought to be fight for their Country . I shall sound he about that stuff to - morrow . if he be one of that lot , he will not   come here with my good - will , I can assure he . what time be he generally to be find down there ? he be right over Stubbard 's head , I believe , and yet friend Adam know nothing about he . nor even Mrs. Adam ! I should have think that worthy pair would have draw any badger in the kingdom . I suppose the youth will see I , if I call . I do not   want to go round that way for nothing . I do want to have a quiet day at home , and saunter in the garden , as the weather be so mild , and consult poor swipe about Spring crop , and then have a pipe or two , and take my gun to Brown Bushes for a woodcock , or a hare , and come home with a fine appetite to a good dinner . but I never must hope for a bit of pleasure now . " 
 " you may depend upon it , sir , " say Frank , " that Caryl Carne will be greatly pleased to see you . and I think you will agree with I that a more straightforward and simple - minded man be not to be find in this country . he combine what we be pleased to call our national dignity and self - respect with the elegant manner , and fraternal warmth , and bonhomie as they themselves express it of our friend across the water . " 
 " you be off ! I do not   want to be cross any more . two hundred thousand friend there at this moment eager to burn down our home and cut our throat ! tired as I be , I ought to take a stick to you , as friend Tugwell do to his son for much less . I have the great mind not to go near that young man . I wish I have Twemlow here to talk it over . pay your fine for a french word , and be off ! " 
 Frank Darling gravely lay down five shilling on his dessert plate , and walk off . the fine for a french word in that house , and in hundred of other english house at this patriotic period , be a crown for a gentleman , and a shilling for a lady , the latter not be liable except when gentleman be present . the poet know well that another word on his part would irritate his father to such a degree that no visit would be pay to - morrow to the admirer of the Harmodiad , whose admiration he be long to reward with a series of good dinner . and so he do his utmost to ensure his father 's visit . 
 but when the Admiral , go warily because he be so stiff from saddle - work make his way down to the house of Widow Shanks , and wink at the Royal Arms in the low front window , where Stubbard keep Office and convenience , knock with the knocker at the private door , there seem to be a great deal of thought require before anybody come to answer . 
 " susie , " say the visitor , who have an especial knack of remember Christian name , which endear he to the bearer , " I be come to see Mr. Carne , and I hope he be at home . " 
 " no , that ' a bain't , sir , " the little girl make answer , after look at the Admiral as if he be an elephant , and wipe her nose with unwonted diligence ; " he be go away , sir ; and please , sir , mother say so . " 
 " well , here be a penny for you , my dear , because you be the good little needle - woman in the school , they tell I . run and tell your mother to come and see I . oh , Mrs. Shanks , I be very glad to see you , and so bloom in spite of all your hard work . ah , it be no easy thing in these hard time to maintain a large family and keep the pot boiling . and everything clean as a quarter - deck ! my certy , you be a woman in a thousand ! " 
 " no , sir , no . it be all the Lord 's doing . and you to the back of he , as I alway say . not a penny can they make out as I owe justly , bad as I be at the figure , squire . do ' e come in , and sit down , there be a dear . ah , I mind the time when you be like a dart , Squire ! " 
 " well , and now I be like a cannon - ball , " say the Admiral , who understand and like this unflattering talk ; " only I do not   travel quite so fast as that . I scarcely get time to see any old friend . but I come to look out for a young friend now , the gentleman you make so comfortable upstairs . do not   I wish I be a young man without incumbrance , to come and lodge with such a wonderful landlady ! " 
 " ah , if there be more of your sort , sir , there would be a deal less trouble in the world , there would . not that my young gentleman be troublesome , mind you , only so full of they outlandish furrin way abideth all day long without ate ort , so different from a honest englishman . first I use to think as he could not   afford it , and long to send he up a bit of my own dinner , but dursn't   for the life of I too grand for that , by ever so till one day little Susie there come a - run down the stair , and she sing out , with her face as red as ever a boil lobster : ' Looky see , mother ! oh , do ' e come and looky see ! Pollyon hath get a heap of guineas on his table ; would not   go into the big yellow pudding - basin ! ' and sure enough he have , your Honour , in pile , as if he be tell of they . he have slip out suddenly , and think the passage door be bolt . what a comfort it be to I , I can not   configurate . because I could eat my dinner comfortable now , for such a big heap of money never I do see . " 
 " I be very glad heartily glad , " exclaim the smile Admiral . " I hope he may get cash enough to buy back all the great Carne property , and kick out those rascally Jews and lawyer . but what make Susie call he that ? " 
 " well , sir , the young one must have a nickname for anything beyond they ; and because he never take any notice of they so different from your handsome Master Frank and some simility of his black horse , or his proud walk , to the pictur ' , ' Pollyon ' be the name they give he , out of Pilgrim 's Progress . though not a bit like he , for such a gentleman to pay his rent and keep his place untroublesome I never have before . and a fortnight he pay I last night , afore go , and take away the key of all three door . " 
 " he be go , then , be he ? to London , I dare say . it would be useless to look for he at the castle . my son will be disappoint more than I be . to tell you the truth , Mrs. Shanks , in these day the great thing be to stick to the people that we know . the world be so full , not of rogue , but of people who be always want something out of one , that to talk with a thoroughly kind , honest person , like yourself , be a real luxury . when the gentleman come back , let he know that I have call . " 
 " and my Jenny , sir ? " cry the anxious mother , run after he into the passage ; " not a word have you say about my Jenny . I hope she show no sign of flightiness ? " 
 " Jenny be as steady as the church , " reply the Admiral . " we be go to put she on a pound a year from next quarter - day , by Mrs. Cloam 's advice . she will have a good stocking by the time she get marry . " 
 " there never be such a pleasant gentleman , nor such a kind - hearted one , I do believe , " say Widow Shanks , as she come in with bright eye . " what be they Carnes to the Darlings , after all ? as different as night and day . " 
 but the Admiral 's next visit be not quite so pleasant ; for when he get back into the village road , expect a nice walk to his luncheon and his pipe , a man run furiously almost knock he down , and have no time to beg his pardon . the runner 's hat be off his head , and his hair blow out , but luckily for itself his tongue be not between his tooth . 
 " have the devil get hold of you at last , Jem Prater ? " the Admiral ask , not profanely ; for he have see a good deal of mankind , and believe in diabolical possession . 
 " for Parson ! for Parson ! " cry Jem , start off again as hard as he could go . " Butter Cheeseman hath hang his self in his own scale . and nobody be any good but Parson . " 
 Admiral Darling be much disturbed . " what will the world come to ? I never know such time , " he exclaim to himself , with some solemnity ; and then set off , as fast as his overridden state permit , for the house of Mr. Cheeseman . pass through the shop , which have nobody in it , he be lead by the sound of voice into a little room beyond it the room in which Mr. Cheeseman have first receive Caryl Carne . here he behold an extraordinary scene , of which he often have to dream thereafter . 
 from a beam in the roof   which have nothing to do with his scale , as Jem Prater have imagine , by a long but not well - plait cord , be dangle the respected Church - warden Cheeseman . happily for he , he have rely on his own good ; and the rope be therefore of very bad hemp , have fail in this sad and too practical proof . the weight of its vendor have add to its length some fifteen inch as he love to pull out thing and his toe touch the floor , which relieve he now and then . 
 " why do not   you cut he down , you old fool ? " cry the Admiral to three gaffer , who stand moralise , while Mrs. Cheeseman sit upon a barrel , sob heavily , with both hand spread to conceal the sad sight . 
 " we be afraid of hurt of he , " say the quick - witted of the gaffer ; " we want to know why ' a do it , " say the deep ; and , " the will of the Lord must be do , " say the wise . 
 after fumble in vain for his knife , and look round , the Admiral run back into the shop , and catch up the sharp steel blade with which the victim of a troubled mind have often unsold a sell ounce in the day of happy commerce . in a moment the Admiral have the poor Church - warden in his sturdy arm , and with a sailor 's skill have unknotte the choke noose , and be shout for brandy , as he keep the blue head from fall back . 
 when a little of the fine eau de vie that ever be smuggle have be administer , the patient rally , and become comparatively cheerful , be enable to explain that " it be all a mistake altogether . " this remove all misunderstanding ; but Rector Twemlow , arrive too late for anything but exhortation , ask a little too sternly as everybody feel under what influence of the Evil One Cheeseman have commit that mistake . the reply be worthy of an enterprising tradesman , and bring he such order from a score of mile around that the resource of the establishment could only book they . 
 " Sir , " he say , look at the parson sadly , with his right hand lay upon his heart , which be feeble , and his left hand intimate that his neck be sore , " if anything have happen that have well not have be , it must have be by reason of the weight I give , and the value such a deal above the price . " 




 CHAPTER XXXVIII 

 EVERYBODY 's MASTER 

 the peril of England be now grow fast ; all the fast from be in the dark . the real design of the enemy escape the penetration even of Nelson , and our Government show more anxiety about their great adversary land on the coast of Egypt than on that of England . naval man laugh at his flat - bottom boat , and declare that one frigate could sink a hundred of they ; whereas it be probable that two of they , with their powerful gun and level fire , would have sink any frigate we then possess . but the crafty and far - see foe do not mean to allow any frigate , or line - of - battle ship , the chance of enquire how that might be . 
 his true scheme , as everybody now know well , be to send the english fleet upon a wild - goose chase , whether to Egypt , the west coast of Ireland , or the West Indies , as the case might be ; and then , by a rapid concentration of his ship , to obtain command of the English Channel , if only for twenty - four hour at a time . twenty - four hour of clearance from our cruiser would have see a hundred thousand man land on our coast , throw up entrenchment , and cover the landing of another hundred thousand , come close upon their heel . who would have face they ? a few good regiment , badly find , and perhaps bad lead , and a mob of militia and raw volunteer , the reward of whose courage would be carnage . 
 but as a chip smell like the tree , and a hair like the dog it belong to , so Springhaven be a very fair sample of the England whereof   in its own opinion   it form a most important part . contempt for the body of a man lead rashly to an under - estimate of his mind ; and one of the great man that ever grow on earth if greatness can be without goodness be hold in low account because not of high inch , and laugh at as " little Boney . " 
 however , there be , as there always be , thousand of sensible englishman then ; and rogue have not yet make a wreck of grand Institutions to scramble for what should wash up . abuse exist , as they always must ; but the great abuse of all   the destruction of every good usage   be undreame of yet . and the right man be even now approach to the rescue , the great Prime - Minister of any age or country . 
 Unwitting perhaps of the fine time afford by the feeble delay of Mr. Addington , and absorb in the tissue of plot and counterplot now thicken fast in Paris the arch - plotter in all of they be himself the First Consul have slacken awhile his hot haste to set foot upon the shore of England . his bottomless ambition for the moment have a top , and that top be the crown of France ; and as soon as he have get that on his head , the head would have no rest until the crown be that of Europe . 
 but before any crown could be put on at all , the tender heart of frenchman must be touch by the appearance of great danger the danger which be of all the great , that to their near and dearest self . a bloody farce be in preparation , noble life be to be perjure away , and above all , the only great rival in the heart of soldier must be turn out of France . this foul job work as foul radical job do now for the good of England . if the french invasion have come to pass , as it be fully mean to do , in the month of February , 1804 , perhaps its history must have be write in French , for we to understand it . 
 so , at any rate , think Caryl Carne , who know the resource of either side , and the difference between a fine army and a mob . he feel quite sure that his mother 's country would conquer his father 's without much trouble , and he know that his horn would be exalt in the land , when he have guide the conqueror into it . sure enough then he would recover his ancestral property with interest and be able to punish his enemy well , and reward his friend if they deserve it . think of these thing , and believe that his own preparation would soon be finish , he leave Widow Shanks to proclaim his merit , while under the bold and able conduct of Captain Renaud Charron he run the gauntlet of the english fleet , and be put ashore southward of Cape Grisnez . here be a long reach of dreary exposure , face the west unprofitably , with a shallow slope of brown sand , and a scour of tide , and no pleasant mooring . jot as the coast be all along   whereon dry battery grin defiance , or sand just awash smile treachery   with shallow transport , gun - boat , prame , scow , bilander , brig , and schooner , row - galley , lugger , and every sort of craft that have a mast , or get on without one , and even a few good ship of war ponder malice in the safe roadstead , yet here the sweep of the west wind , and the long roll from the ocean following , keep a league or two , northward of the mighty defence of Boulogne , inviolate by the petty enmity of man . along the slight curve of the coast might be see , beyond Ambleteuse and Wimereux , the vast extent of the french flotilla , range in three division , before the great lunette of the central camp , and hill jot with tent thick as limpet on a rock .
